title: MCEN3022: Heat Transfer
text:
Home
Welcome to MCEN 3022: Heat Transfer! This course is being taught in summer 2026 by Professor Jeremy Koch at the University of Colorado Boulder.
This page contains some perspective and important policies.

title: MCEN3022: Heat Transfer
section: Some things to look forward to
text:
Some things to look forward to

Heat Transfer is a delightfully practical course where we get to do some genuine engineering analyses. Problems like: “A GPU produces 800 W of waste heat and needs to be kept below 80°C. If we air cool it, how fast must the fan be? If we water cool it, how high must the flow rate be?”
Despite the asynchronous nature of this course, we are going to have some technical discussions over Zoom. These will be some of the most authentic engineering experiences you will have here at CU, with you and a small number of colleagues getting together to talk through a problem for ~30 minutes.
We will “use AI the right way” and get some experience with Google’s NotebookLM. I envision this as being a useful tool for your other courses, maybe most significantly your senior design course, and in your career, so let’s get some practice with it this semester.

title: MCEN3022: Heat Transfer
text:
Class Expectations

This is an 8-week course that will cover the same amount of content as in a regular semester with 15 weeks. Maybe most worryingly, there are only seven weekends – very little chance to recover! Due to the fast pace of this course, you cannot fall behind – there will be no late work accepted and no extensions.
This is also an asynchronous course, meaning that you will consume the course content on your own schedule. This will require self-motivation to stay on track! (There are due dates, and there are some events that you will need to schedule.)
In a regular semester we would see each other 150 minutes per week, plus office hours (always a good idea to attend). Since this course is half as long, does that mean that you will be watching 300 minutes of videos per week? No!

I am making an effort to streamline the videos – fewer umms, pauses, and stumbles. Derivations will mostly be placed on this website, read them there.
I expect you will pause, think, rewind, and revisit other sections of your notes. A 15-minute video might take 25 minutes to consume.
This website is meant to augment the lecture content and contains abbreviated descriptions, derivations, equation and algorithm summaries, interactive examples, and some quick “check your understanding” questions. You should be reading the website to review/solidify the content.

Your work, particularly the “technical reports”, will be neat and professional. Creating detailed, readable reports is an engineering skill!

title: MCEN3022: Heat Transfer
text:
Workflow
I recommend the following: - This website will be the guide, but we are required to use Canvas for the gradebook, homework submissions, etc.

title: MCEN3022: Heat Transfer
text:
Assessment components
The course is designed as a connected system, not a collection of independent assignments:

Tier 1 problems build the fluency you need for the technical reports
Technical reports create documents you will defend in the oral exams
Peer discussions give you practice reasoning out loud before the oral exams
NotebookLM helps you build a retrievable knowledge base you can bring to the oral exams
Midterm oral is a calibration point — you find out where you stand while there is still time to improve
Capstone + final oral is where everything comes together

Missing any one of these components does not just cost you points on that component. It makes the downstream components harder. See Assessment Overview & Philosophy for more details on these.

title: Assessment Overview & Philosophy
text:
Why This Course Is Designed the Way It Is
Traditional engineering course assessments — homework sets, midterms, finals — were designed for a world where producing a correct solution required understanding the material. That assumption is no longer reliable. AI tools can generate technically correct solutions, clean derivations, and plausible physical interpretations.
This creates an unfairness that is easy to overlook: in a traditional grading scheme, a student who works through every problem honestly and a student who outsources everything to AI may receive the same homework grade. That is not fair to the student who worked honestly.
This course is designed so that real understanding is visible and rewarded. The primary mechanism is simple: a significant portion of your grade comes from real-time conversation — oral exams and peer discussions — where understanding either shows up or it does not.

title: Assessment Overview & Philosophy
text:
Grade Breakdown




Component
Weight
Authenticity
Primary Purpose




NotebookLM portfolio
10%




Tier 1 problems
10%




Technical reports
15%




Peer discussions
20%




Midterm oral
15%




Capstone report
5%




Final oral
25%

title: Assessment Overview & Philosophy
section: What Each Component Is Actually For
text:
What Each Component Is Actually For
Tier 1 problems exist because fluency requires repetition. You cannot reason well about transient conduction in a fin if you have never worked a conduction problem under time pressure. These problems build the background knowledge that makes the oral exam feel natural rather than overwhelming.
Technical reports exist to force you to write down your reasoning in a form that can be interrogated. The report is not the product — the oral exam is. But you cannot have a good oral exam conversation without a document to point at. The report also builds professional writing habits that matter beyond this course.
Peer discussions exist because engineering happens in teams. Explaining your reasoning to a peer, in real time, without notes, is a different cognitive act than writing it down. It is also excellent preparation for the oral exam.
Oral exams exist because they are the most direct window into understanding. You will be asked to explain your reasoning, defend your choices, and respond to “what if” questions that were not on any assignment. These conversations cannot be prepared for by memorizing solutions — only by understanding the material.
The capstone exists to require synthesis. Heat transfer problems in practice do not come labeled by mode. You will be given an open-ended system, asked to identify what matters, build an appropriate model, and justify every choice.

title: Assessment Overview & Philosophy
text:
A Word on Oral Exams
Oral exams are uncommon in undergraduate engineering programs, and many students find them anxiety-inducing at first. A few things worth knowing:
The exam is not designed to trick you or be a “gotcha”. It is a conversation about material you have been working with for weeks. Stumbling on a question and recovering is fine – recovery is part of what we are watching for. Getting something wrong and correcting yourself is better than getting it right the first time with no visible reasoning.
The anchor question for the midterm oral will be released one week in advance. You will know exactly what the opening question is. The follow-up questions draw from your own submitted reports — material you wrote yourself.
You may bring your NotebookLM notebook to both oral exams.
A retake of the midterm oral is available. See Oral Exams for details.

title: Tier 1 Practice Problems
text:
Overview
Tier 1 problems are practice. There are approximately 20 of them distributed across the course, covering the full range of course content. They vary in type — some are conceptual (“why is there a negative sign in Fourier’s law?”), some are quantitative (“find the heat flux through this composite wall”), and some are analytical (“derive the fin efficiency for these boundary conditions”).
What they have in common: they do not require a full technical report. A clean solution with units, a clearly stated answer, and one sentence of physical interpretation is sufficient.

title: Tier 1 Practice Problems
text:
Why These Problems Exist
Fluency requires repetition. You cannot reason well about transient conduction in a fin if you have never worked a conduction problem under time pressure. These problems build the background knowledge and physical intuition that make the oral exam feel natural rather than overwhelming.
They are worth 10% of your grade and graded primarily on completion. That is not because they are unimportant — it is because the point is the doing, not the product. The oral exam is where the doing gets verified.

These problems are worth 10% and graded on completion. That is not where the value is. The value is that students who work them find the oral exam straightforward. Students who do not find it very uncomfortable. The math is designed to be obvious.

title: Tier 1 Practice Problems
text:
Submission Format
Each Tier 1 problem follows the same two-stage process as the technical reports:
Stage 1 (Thursday, 11:59 PM): Submit a genuine attempt. Legible work, units carried, answer stated. Completion earns full credit for this stage. Submitting Stage 1 unlocks the Stage 2 assignment, which includes milestone checkpoints.
Stage 2 (Sunday, 11:59 PM): Submit a clean final version. This is lightly graded on completion and correctness — the goal is a document you could hand to a colleague and they could follow your work.

title: Tier 1 Practice Problems
section: What a Good Tier 1 Submission Looks Like
text:
What a Good Tier 1 Submission Looks Like

Work is legible and organized
Units are carried through every line
Answer is clearly stated
One sentence explains the physical meaning of the result: not just “Q = 245 W” but “Q = 245 W, which is consistent with the expected direction of heat flow from hot to cold”

That last sentence is often the most valuable part. It is also the hardest to write without understanding the problem — which is precisely the point.

title: Tier 1 Practice Problems
text:
Grading








Stage
Weight
Basis




Stage 1 (initial)
Completion
Genuine attempt submitted on time


Stage 2 (final)
Completion + light correctness
Clean solution, units, answer, physical interpretation



TAs will return these quickly. Feedback will be brief — a flag if something is physically wrong, otherwise a check.

title: Tier 1 Practice Problems
text:
A Note on AI
You can use AI to generate a Tier 1 solution. The completion grade does not depend on how you produced it.
What it does depend on: the oral exam. A student who worked the problems will find the oral exam’s follow-up questions familiar. A student who did not will find them uncomfortable in proportion to how many problems they skipped. There is no penalty attached to this observation — it is simply how the course works.

title: Technical Reports
text:
Overview
You will submit 5-6 technical reports over the course of the semester. These are full engineering analyses — not just solutions, but professional documents that justify every assumption, explain every modeling choice, and interrogate every result.
The reports are not graded primarily on polish. They are graded on the quality of your physical reasoning and your ability to catch and correct your own mistakes. A messy report that shows genuine reasoning scores higher than a clean report that could have been produced without understanding the problem.
Why? Because the oral exam is where report quality truly gets assessed. You will be asked to walk through your reasoning, defend your choices, and answer follow-up questions drawn directly from what you wrote. A report you did not genuinely produce is a liability in the oral exam, not an asset.

title: Technical Reports
text:
The Two-Stage Submission Process
Each technical report has two submissions, both managed through Canvas.

Stage 1: Initial Submission (Thursday, 11:59 PM)
Submit a genuine attempt at the problem. This does not need to be polished — it needs to be honest. Legible work, units carried, an attempt at the key physics.
Grading: Completion only. A genuine attempt earns full credit for this stage.
What unlocks: Submitting Stage 1 immediately unlocks the Stage 2 assignment, which includes milestone checkpoints for the problem. These checkpoints confirm whether your approach is correct and flag common errors. You do not need to wait until Sunday to access them — submit Stage 1 early in the week and you have the checkpoints immediately.


Stage 2: Final Submission (Sunday, 11:59 PM)
A complete technical report following the structure below. This is what is graded on the full rubric.
The reflection section (see below) asks you to describe specifically what you got wrong or nearly got wrong in your Stage 1 submission and how you caught and corrected it. This section requires a genuine Stage 1 attempt — it cannot be written without one.

title: Technical Reports
text:
Report Structure
Every technical report should follow this structure:

Problem Statement & Assumptions (~½ page) Restate the problem in your own words. List and justify every assumption. “Steady state” is not a justification — “steady state because the problem specifies constant boundary conditions and we are not asked about transient behavior” is.
Approach & Solution Method (~½–1 page) Why this method? Explain the physical reasoning behind your modeling choices. What did you consider and reject? Why does this approach capture the important physics?
Solution Equations numbered and referenced in text. Units carried throughout every line. No orphaned equations.
Results & Sanity Checks (~½ page) Does your answer make sense? Check order of magnitude, limiting behavior, units. Compare to intuition or a simpler case. An answer that passes a sanity check earns more credit than the same answer without one.
Common Pitfall & Correction (~¼ page) What assumption or approach did you initially get wrong or nearly get wrong? How did you detect the error — did units not work out, did a sanity check fail, did a limiting case give a nonsensical result? What did you do to correct it?
This section is one of the most important in the report. Every engineer makes wrong assumptions. The skill is catching them. A strong response to this section is better preparation for the oral exam than a perfect first attempt would have been.
Reflection (~¼ page) What was the hardest part of this problem? What connects to real engineering systems or other course material? What would you want to know more about?

title: Technical Reports
text:
Grading Rubric
Each final submission is graded on 8 points across three dimensions.

Physics Reasoning (4 points)







Score
Description




4
Physical reasoning complete and followable throughout. All assumptions stated and justified. Sanity check attempted with genuine physical interpretation. Units carried throughout.


3
Reasoning mostly present. Minor gaps — one assumption unjustified, sanity check cursory, or isolated unit lapse.


2
Reasoning partially present. Significant gap: major assumption unjustified, sanity check missing, or solution presented without connecting physical logic.


1
Minimal reasoning. Solution may be correct but student cannot be credited with understanding why.


0
No physical reasoning visible. Could have been copied without understanding.





Common Pitfall & Correction (2 points)







Score
Description




2
Specific error or near-miss identified. Detection method explained clearly (units, sanity check, limiting case, etc.). Correction described. Consistent with Stage 1 submission.


1
Reflection present but vague, generic (“I made a math error”), or partially inconsistent with Stage 1 submission.


0
Section absent, or clearly inconsistent with Stage 1 submission in a way that suggests Stage 1 was not a genuine attempt.





Presentation (2 points)







Score
Description




2
Report follows required structure. Equations numbered and referenced in text. Writing is clear and followable throughout.


1
Mostly organized but with missing elements — unreferenced equations, unclear section, or writing that impedes understanding in places.


0
Missing sections, or writing that consistently impedes understanding.



Total: 8 points per report

title: Technical Reports
text:
What TAs Are Looking For
When TAs read your report, they are asking one question above all others: can I tell that this student understands why they did what they did?
A technically correct solution with no visible reasoning scores lower than a partially correct solution with clear physical reasoning. The oral exam is where correctness is verified — the report is where reasoning is demonstrated.
TA feedback on draft submissions will focus on reasoning gaps, not on polish. If your reasoning is incomplete, you will be told specifically where and why.

title: Technical Reports
text:
Connection to Oral Exams
Your submitted reports become the text interrogated in the oral exams. Expect questions like:

“You wrote X on page 2 — walk me through that reasoning.”
“You assumed Y here — what would change if that assumption didn’t hold?”
“Your sanity check gave Z — is that what you expected, and why?”
“You said in your pitfall section that you initially got the boundary condition wrong — how did you catch that?”

The best preparation for these questions is writing the report yourself.

title: Oral Exams
text:
Overview
There are two oral exams in this course: a midterm oral (~Week 4) and a final oral (~Week 7-8). Together with the capstone report, they account for 52% of your course grade.
Oral exams are uncommon in undergraduate engineering programs. This page explains how they work, what is expected, and how to prepare.

title: Oral Exams
text:
Why Oral Exams
The short answer: they are the most direct window into understanding that exists in a course setting.
A written solution can be produced without understanding the reasoning behind it. A conversation cannot. When you are asked to explain why you made a modeling choice, what would change if a parameter shifted, or where your solution would break down — the answer either comes from genuine understanding or it does not. There is no middle ground.
This is not about catching anyone out. It is about making the grade mean something. A student who earns a strong grade in this course has demonstrated, in real time, that they can reason about heat transfer. That is a credential worth having.

title: Oral Exams
text:
Format
Both oral exams follow the same three-part structure:

Part 1: Anchor Question (~4-5 minutes)
A conceptual question, the same for every student, released one week before the exam. You will know exactly what this question is before you sit down.
Example: “Walk me through transient conduction in a semi-infinite solid — what is happening physically, what are the key parameters, and what does the solution tell you?”
The anchor question is released in advance to reduce anxiety and reward preparation, not to make the exam easy. A strong answer demonstrates organized conceptual understanding. A weak answer — even on a question you had a week to prepare — tells us something important.


Part 2: Report Defense (~5-7 minutes)
The examiner will draw from one of your submitted technical reports. You will be asked to walk through your reasoning, justify your assumptions, and explain your sanity checks.
Expect questions like:

“You wrote X on page 2 — walk me through that reasoning.”
“You assumed Y here. What would change if that assumption didn’t hold?”
“Your pitfall section says you initially got the boundary condition wrong. How did you catch that?”

The report you defend will be drawn from your submitted work up to that point. For the midterm, this means reports submitted through approximately Week 3. You will not be told in advance which report will be discussed.


Part 3: Extension Question (~3-5 minutes)
A “what if” question that modifies a parameter or removes an assumption from the problem you just discussed.
Example: “You found the steady-state temperature distribution assuming constant thermal conductivity. What would change if conductivity varied with temperature?”
This part of the exam cannot be prepared for by memorizing solutions. It can only be prepared for by understanding the physics well enough to reason about perturbations.
Total time: approximately 12-17 minutes per student.

title: Oral Exams
text:
Midterm Oral Exam (~Week 4)
Weight: 20% of course grade
The midterm oral is primarily formative — it is a calibration point that tells you where you stand while there is still time to improve. A weak midterm performance is recoverable.
You may bring your NotebookLM notebook to the exam.

Retake Policy
A retake of the midterm oral is available to any student who wants one.
How it works:

The retake score replaces the original score — for better or worse. If you do better, your grade improves. If you do worse, your grade drops. This is intentional: the retake is for students who are genuinely better prepared, not for students who are hoping for an easier question.
The retake uses a different anchor question and draws from a wider report pool. It is designed to be comparable in difficulty, not easier.
To schedule a retake, you must first submit a short written reflection (see below).

Retake reflection (required to schedule):
Submit a paragraph — 3-5 sentences — addressing the following:

What specific concept or question in the exam did you struggle with, and why?
What have you done since the exam to address that gap?
What are you prepared to demonstrate in the retake that you could not demonstrate the first time?

Vague reflections (“I didn’t study enough”) will be returned without scheduling a retake. Specific reflections (“I couldn’t explain why the Biot number assumption broke down in the composite wall problem, so I reworked problems 2 and 4 from the Tier 1 set”) will be scheduled promptly.
The reflection is not graded. It is a commitment device — it filters for students who have actually done additional preparation, and it gives the examiner useful information going into the retake.

title: Oral Exams
text:
Final Oral Exam (~Week 7-8)
Weight: Approximately 22% of course grade (70% of the combined 32% capstone + final oral weight)
The final oral is high-stakes. It interrogates your capstone report deeply and tests your ability to synthesize across the full course.

Structure







Time
Content




0-3 min
Student gives a 2-minute unprompted summary of their capstone system and findings


3-10 min
Examiner probes mechanism identification and modeling choices from capstone


10-16 min
“What if” questions — change a parameter, remove an assumption, add a mode


16-20 min
Student reflection — what would you do differently, what are you least confident in



The final oral differs from the midterm in one important way: the last four minutes are yours. You are asked to identify the weakest part of your own work. This is not a trap — an engineer who can articulate the limitations of their own analysis is more valuable than one who cannot.
You may bring your NotebookLM notebook to the exam.

title: Oral Exams
text:
How to Prepare
The most effective preparation is doing the work throughout the course. Students who engage genuinely with Tier 1 problems, write their own technical reports, and participate actively in peer discussions consistently find oral exams manageable. Students who do not find them very uncomfortable.
Beyond that:

Practice explaining out loud. Writing and speaking are different cognitive acts. Talk through your solutions to a roommate, a study group, or yourself.
Know your reports. You wrote them — you should be able to walk through any part of them without looking at the document.
Prepare your anchor question seriously. You have a week. A well-organized, physically motivated answer to the anchor question sets a strong tone for everything that follows.
Build your NotebookLM. Students who have rich, well-organized notebooks use them fluently in the exam. Students who built them poorly find them unhelpful under pressure.

title: Oral Exams
text:
Logistics
Exams are conducted by the instructor and TAs via Zoom. Scheduling windows will be posted in Canvas approximately two weeks before each exam period. Sign up early — late slots fill last and create unnecessary deadline pressure.
Each exam is approximately 12-17 minutes. Plan for 20 minutes including brief setup and transition time.

title: Peer Discussions
text:
Overview
You will participate in 5-6 recorded peer technical discussions over the course of the semester. Each session is conducted via Zoom in rotating groups of 4-6 students, lasts 30-60 minutes, and centers on a novel problem you work through together in real time.
Peer discussions are worth 18% of your course grade — more than the technical reports. This reflects what they actually test: the ability to reason out loud, in real time, with other engineers, about a problem you have not seen before. That is one of the most authentic engineering skills this course can develop, and one of the hardest things to fake.

title: Peer Discussions
text:
Why Peer Discussions
Writing a technical report gives you time to look things up, revise your reasoning, and present a polished product. A peer discussion gives you none of that. You have to engage with the physics as it unfolds, respond to your peers’ ideas, catch errors in real time, and contribute something that moves the group forward.
This is also excellent preparation for the oral exams. Students who participate actively in peer discussions consistently find the oral exam format less surprising.

title: Peer Discussions
text:
Format
Group size: 4-6 students, rotating across sessions so you work with different peers throughout the course
Duration: 30-60 minutes per session
Problem: A novel problem released at the start of the session — one you have not seen before, but drawn from material already covered in the course
Recording: Sessions are recorded via Zoom for assessment purposes. You are expected to state your name clearly at the start of the session to support accurate transcript labeling.
What you are expected to do: Work through the problem together. Explain your reasoning, respond to others’ ideas, ask probing questions, catch errors, and push the group toward a physically sound solution. You are not expected to arrive at a perfect answer — you are expected to reason well.
Final 5 minutes: In the last 5 minutes of each session, the group should converge on a collective answer to the core question: what is the dominant heat transfer mechanism, what model would you use, and what are your key assumptions? This verbal summary will be captured in the transcript and is factored into individual assessments. No separate submission is required.

title: Peer Discussions
text:
How You Are Assessed
After each session, an AI language model analyzes the discussion transcript and generates an individual assessment report for each student. You receive your report within 24-48 hours of the session.

The Rubric (1-4 scale)








Score
Label
Description




4
Leading
Drove physical reasoning; introduced key concepts; resolved confusion; asked probing questions that advanced the group


3
Contributing
Made at least one substantive physical contribution the group built on


2
Present
Engaged but mostly social or confirmatory — agreed with others, restated ideas, but did not add physical substance


1
Peripheral
Spoke rarely; contributions did not advance physical reasoning



The key principle: substance over quantity. A single probing question that reorients the group’s reasoning scores higher than ten confirmatory statements. Talking frequently is not the same as contributing substantively. The assessment is calibrated to reflect this.


What Your Report Contains
Your individual assessment report includes:

Your key physics contributions, with direct quotes from the transcript
An evaluation of your reasoning quality — whether your contributions reflected genuine understanding or were vague and surface-level
Your discussion role — driver, responder, or peripheral
Your score (1-4)
Any uncertainty flags — places where the transcript was ambiguous about attribution or intent



Transparency
The full system prompt used to instruct the AI assessor is shared with you at the beginning of the course. You will know exactly what the AI is looking for, what it is instructed to reward, and what it is instructed to flag. There are no hidden criteria.
This is intentional. The assessment is not trying to catch you out — it is trying to accurately characterize your contribution to the group’s physical reasoning.


A Note on Incorrect Statements
The AI is instructed to note physically incorrect statements even when delivered confidently. Getting something wrong in a discussion is not penalized heavily — reasoning through an error and correcting it can be a substantive contribution. Confidently stating something incorrect without correction is noted.

title: Peer Discussions
text:
The Contestation Process
If you believe your assessment report is inaccurate, you may contest it.
Window: 3-5 days after receiving your report
How to contest: Submit a written response that identifies specifically which part of the assessment you disagree with and why, with reference to the transcript if possible. (“I believe my contribution at approximately 14:30 was substantive because it introduced the Biot number criterion that the group had not yet applied” is a useful contestation. “I think I deserved a higher score” is not.)
What happens: A TA reads the transcript, your report, and your contestation, and makes a final determination. The TA’s decision is final.
Why this process exists: AI assessment is imperfect. Transcripts have attribution errors. Ambiguous statements get miscategorized. The contestation process is a real mechanism, not a formality — if your report is wrong, this is how you correct it.

title: Peer Discussions
section: This Is Also a Research Study
text:
This Is Also a Research Study
The peer discussion assessment system is part of an IRB-approved research study examining how well AI-generated assessments agree with human expert ratings, and how students perceive transparent AI grading.
Participation in the research component is optional and separate from participation in the course activity. You participate in the discussions and receive assessments regardless of whether you consent to research participation. Research participation means your transcript and assessment data may be used in a published study — with all identifying information removed.
You will receive more information about the research study and the consent process in Week 1.
Whether or not you participate in the research, the assessment process is identical.

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
text:
Overview
NotebookLM is a free AI tool from Google that retrieves and synthesizes content from documents you upload. Unlike general AI tools like ChatGPT, it can only draw from sources you provide — it cannot generate answers from general knowledge. If the answer is not in your notes, NotebookLM cannot give it to you.
You will use NotebookLM throughout this course to organize and query your lecture notes. The NotebookLM portfolio is worth X% of your course grade and is submitted at two checkpoints: Week 4 (formative feedback only) and Week 8 (graded).

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
text:
Why NotebookLM
Three reasons this tool is part of the course:
First, it encourages better notes. Heat transfer is highly cumulative — material from Week 1 appears in problems in Week 6. A well-organized, searchable notebook makes that cumulative structure manageable rather than overwhelming.
Second, it supports engagement in an asynchronous course. NotebookLM is only as good as the notes you put into it. Weak notes produce a weak notebook. The portfolio submission is partly a check that you are actually watching lectures and engaging with the material.
Third, it models appropriate AI use. There is a meaningful difference between an AI tool that retrieves your own understanding and one that generates answers you do not have. NotebookLM cannot do your thinking for you — it can only help you access thinking you have already done. That is a useful distinction, and one that generalizes beyond this course.

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
text:
How NotebookLM Works

Create a free notebook at notebooklm.google.com using your CU Boulder account. (We get a slightly more robust version when we login using CU credentials.)
Upload your lecture notes as sources: PDFs, Google Docs, or text files work well. If you want to talk about preparing your notes using Markdown or LaTeX, let me know and I can prepare a tutorial!
Query your notebook using natural language: “What is the physical significance of the Biot number?” or “Show me my notes on fin efficiency”.
NotebookLM synthesizes relevant content from your uploaded sources and cites which source it drew from.

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
text:
Setup (Week 1)
There is a course video for Week 1 (~15 minutes) that walks you through NotebookLM.

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
text:
Portfolio Submission
The portfolio has two components, submitted together at each checkpoint.

Query Log
A curated list of 10-15 queries you found most useful, with brief annotations. For each query:

The query you submitted
A 1-2 sentence annotation explaining why this query was useful — what it helped you retrieve, clarify, or connect

The goal is not a comprehensive log of every query you ran. It is a curated selection that demonstrates you used the tool in a way that supported your learning.
Example of a useful annotated entry:

Query: “What assumptions are required for the lumped capacitance method?”
Annotation: This came up when I was working on Report 2 and couldn’t remember the Biot number criterion. The notebook pulled my notes from the Week 3 lecture and reminded me that Bi < 0.1 is the standard threshold, which I had written down but forgotten. Confirmed my approach was valid.

Example of a less useful annotated entry:

Query: “convection”
Annotation: I looked this up to review convection.

The first example shows genuine engagement. The second does not.


Reflection
For each major course unit, 1-2 paragraphs addressing:

What did you search for that your notebook could not answer — and why not?
How did you resolve the gap? (Rewatched a lecture, worked a problem, asked during office hours, found it in the textbook?)

This section is more valuable than the query log in some ways. The gaps in your notebook are a record of where your notes were thin, where a concept didn’t stick, or where you needed a source your notebook didn’t have. Reflecting on how you resolved those gaps is exactly the kind of metacognitive habit that leads to a greater understanding… of your understanding!

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
text:
Grading
This will largely be graded on completion and genuine engagement, not on the sophistication of your notes or of your queries. A notebook built from genuine note-taking with thoughtful queries should earn full marks.
What earns less than full marks: query logs with vague annotations that do not explain what was useful, or reflections that describe no gaps (which suggests either the notebook is not being used or the reflections are not genuine).

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
section: Bringing Your Notebook to Oral Exams
text:
Bringing Your Notebook to Oral Exams
You are strongly encouraged to bring your NotebookLM to both oral exams. You may query it during the exam.
This is an advantage — but only if you built it well. Students with rich, well-organized notebooks find it genuinely useful under pressure. Students who uploaded thin notes find that querying returns thin results at an inconvenient moment.
The oral exam is, among other things, an authenticity check on the portfolio. A student who built a strong notebook uses it fluently. A student who did not built one finds quickly that there is nothing useful to retrieve.

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
section: Some ways of thinking about NotebookLM
text:
Some ways of thinking about NotebookLM
Maybe one of these will speak to you?

NotebookLM can be thought of as an “external brain” that contains the details, while you focus on the engineering process. You know there is a correlation for the Nusselt Number for a particular geometry, and instead of digging through your notes to find it, you prompt your notebook and get started writing out the problem while it thinks. In the 15 seconds it takes to retrieve the relevant equation, you have already written out the assumptions and first step of the solution and are ready to plug-in the equation.
Similarly: you can think of yourself as a senior engineer, and NotebookLM is a junior engineer, and you are working on a project together. Don’t blame the junior engineer if there are bogus results, you trained them!
NotebookLM is a way to check that a human colleague (or maybe you in six months) could get up-to-speed on a project based on your notes. If the AI can’t read your work, or is confused by your organization, or makes some incorrect connections, a human would probably do the same. If your notebook is good, a person could use it to pick up a project easily. Future you will thank you.

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
text:
Some words on note-taking
This aspect of the course is a bit peculiar, particularly for an upper-level course… you get points for taking notes? But you take notes in other classes and don’t get points? We are emphasizing “knowledge management” in this course because it is a skill that is going to become very important to you very soon, with senior design and your career on the horizon. In industry, if your work isn’t documented well, it didn’t happen.
Some students are already taking clean detailed notes - for them, cultivating their notebook will be easier, and they will be less likely to encounter a confused AI… is that an m, or an n? But this isn’t that different from a student who has a stronger background in differential equations: that student will likely have an easier time with the math in this course. Both technical ability and the ability to communicate are being emphasized in this course.

title: NotebookLM Portfolio
text:
AI Policy
NotebookLM is indeed an AI tool, though I believe it is an example of “using AI the right way”. There are right ways to use any AI, but the trouble with LLMs, at least in an educational context, is that they are too eager to just do the work for you. “How can I factor a heat generation rate of \(5x(1-x)\) into my conduction equation?” gets an immediate response: “Here is the solution, and here is the maximum temperature, and here is the heat flux out each wall. Is there anything else I can help you with?” That is not serving your development as an engineer. Officially, general AI use is prohibited in this class, though I am not naive enough to think that policing it is a good use of my time.
There are probably wrong ways to use NotebookLM as well. As we saw in the tutorial video, you can push NotebookLM to do a little math for you. I’m not sure how far this can go… I guess you can explore, but make sure you are the one doing your homework.

title: Capstone Project
text:
Overview
The capstone is the synthesis assessment for the course. It is released approximately Week 5 and due approximately Week 7, giving you two weeks to develop your analysis while you are still encountering new material. The capstone report and the final oral exam together are worth 32% of your course grade.
The capstone is not a harder version of a technical report. It is a different kind of problem entirely.

title: Capstone Project
text:
What Makes the Capstone Different
Every technical report in this course gives you a well-posed problem: a geometry, a set of boundary conditions, a quantity to find. The physics is already identified for you. Your job is to apply the appropriate method correctly and justify your reasoning.
The capstone gives you a real system and a question. You decide which heat transfer mechanisms matter. You decide what to model and what to neglect. You decide how much complexity is warranted and how to check that your model is physically reasonable. There is no single correct answer — there are better and worse justified approaches.
This is closer to what engineering analysis actually looks like.

title: Capstone Project
text:
The Prompt
You will be given a real engineering system and asked something like:

“You are an engineer asked to evaluate [system]. Characterize the dominant heat transfer mechanisms, develop an appropriate model, and provide quantitative estimates with uncertainty. Justify every assumption you make.”

The specific system will be announced when the capstone is released. It will involve multiple heat transfer modes — conduction, convection, and radiation will all be potentially relevant, and part of your task is deciding which ones matter enough to model explicitly and which ones can be reasonably neglected.

title: Capstone Project
text:
Report Structure
The capstone uses the same technical report structure as your earlier reports, with one addition:

Problem Statement & Assumptions — restate the system, identify what you are trying to characterize, list and justify every assumption
Mechanism Identification — which heat transfer modes are present? Which dominate? Why are others negligible? This section is new and central to the capstone.
Approach & Solution Method — why this model? What did you consider and reject?
Solution — equations numbered, units throughout
Results & Sanity Checks — does your answer make sense? What is the uncertainty in your estimates?
Common Pitfall & Correction — what did you initially get wrong or nearly get wrong in your modeling approach?
Reflection — what would you do differently with more time or data? What are you least confident in?

The mechanism identification section is where most capstone analyses succeed or fail. A strong analysis identifies the dominant modes with physical justification and makes a principled argument for why others are negligible. A weak analysis models everything without discrimination, or neglects something important without justification.

title: Capstone Project
text:
Grading
The capstone report is graded on the same 8-point rubric as the technical reports (physics reasoning 4 points, reflection 2 points, presentation 2 points), with additional weight given to the mechanism identification section within the physics reasoning dimension.
The capstone report grade is combined with the final oral exam score to produce the 32% component. The final oral exam carries more weight than the report — approximately 70/30. A strong oral exam can recover a weak report; a weak oral exam reveals the limits of a strong-looking report.

title: Capstone Project
text:
The Final Oral Exam
The final oral exam interrogates your capstone directly. See Oral Exams for the full structure. In brief:

You open with a 2-minute unprompted summary of your system and findings
The examiner probes your mechanism identification and modeling choices
“What if” questions modify your system — a different material, a different operating condition, an additional mode
You close by identifying what you are least confident in and what you would do differently

The last part is not a trap. An engineer who can articulate the limitations of their own analysis is more valuable than one who presents false certainty. The capstone is a document you should be able to defend, critique, and extend.

title: Capstone Project
text:
Advice
Start with the mechanisms, not the math. The most common capstone error is jumping to equations before establishing which physics dominates. Spend real time on mechanism identification before you write a single equation. Order-of-magnitude estimates of each mode’s contribution are more valuable at this stage than precise calculations.
Justify everything. Every assumption you make is a potential oral exam question. “I neglected radiation because…” should appear in your report before the examiner asks it.
Use your NotebookLM. You have been building a notebook for weeks. The capstone is exactly the kind of open-ended synthesis task it is designed to support. Query it for definitions, criteria, and material properties. You can bring it to the final oral exam.
The uncertainty estimate is real. You are working from a described system, not a controlled laboratory. Your inputs have uncertainty. Acknowledging that uncertainty — and reasoning about how it propagates through your analysis — is part of a professional engineering analysis, not a sign of weakness.

title: Intro
text:
Warm-Up
Let’s get started! This is part of your Week 1 content. We will introduce the three modes of heat transfer and introduce some vocabulary that will be used throughout the course.
In general I will try to start off each week with a few questions like these to get the gears turning. The “Recall” questions you should know, and if you don’t, revisit your notes. The “Look Ahead” questions you should not expect to know – the purpose is to prime your brain to seek the answers to those questions in the videos and readings. At the end of the week, revisit these questions.
Recall 1. From thermodynamics: energy can be transferred via heat or work. This class is about heat!

title: Intro
text:
What are the different ways that heat can be transferred?
What are the meanings of thermal conductivity \(k\) and the convective heat transfer coefficient \(h\)? What are their units?
How does the Sun heat up the Earth through the vacuum of space?

title: Intro
section: The Three Modes of Heat Transfer
text:
The Three Modes of Heat Transfer






TipVideo



Start with this video. Then read this section… it will hopefully help you solidify and maybe extend your learning.


From thermodynamics, recall that the energy of a system is changed via heat transfer or work (on or by). The Laws of Thermodynamics say nothing about rates! Heat transfer is the study of the heat transfer rate.
We give the symbol \(\dot{Q}\) to the heat transfer rate, where \(Q\) is the amount of heat (energy), in Joules, and the dot on top references a time derivative.

Conduction
Primarily associated with solids, though fluids have a “thermal conductivity” too (convection is stronger).
\[\dot{Q}_\text{conduction} \sim k \cdot A \cdot \frac{T - T_0}{w}\]








Symbol
Meaning
Units




\(k\)
thermal conductivity (material property)
W/m·K


\(A\)
cross-sectional area
m²


\(T - T_0\)
temperature difference between surfaces separated by width \(w\)
K


\(w\)
width (thickness)
m






Convection
Medium is a fluid (gas or liquid), somehow correlated with speed.
\[\dot{Q}_\text{convection} \sim h \cdot A \cdot (T - T_0)\]








Symbol
Meaning
Units




\(h\)
convective heat transfer coefficient (depends on material, size, speed, …)
W/m²·K


\(A\)
surface area
m²


\(T\)
object temperature
K


\(T_0\)
fluid temperature
K






Radiation
Does not require a medium — transmitted by light/photons.
\[\dot{Q}_\text{radiation} \sim \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot F \cdot A \cdot (T^4 - T_0^4)\]








Symbol
Meaning
Units




\(\varepsilon\)
emissivity — how perfect is “the emitter” (material property, \(0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1\))
—


\(\sigma\)
Stefan-Boltzmann constant
\(5.67 \times 10^{-8}\) W/m²·K⁴


\(F\)
view factor — how well the objects “see” each other
—


\(A\)
surface area
m²


\(T\)
object surface temperature
K


\(T_0\)
surroundings temperature (e.g. \(T_\text{sun}\))
K

title: Intro
text:
Course outline
To give some context for the course flow:

Weeks 1-3 will be about conduction: the conduction equation, the thermal resistance model, fins (one of the most important engineering design strategies), and transient conduction.
Weeks 4-6 will be about convection: boundary layers, the significance of turbulence, flow over objects (external flow), flow through objects (internal flow), and heat exchangers.
Weeks 7-8 will be about radiation: emissivity (a material property), the view factor, and the radiation resistance model.

title: Intro
text:
Some vocabulary

adiabatic

No heat transfer. Mathematically (from Fourier’s Law), \(dT/dx\) at an adiabatic surface is zero.

insulator

A material with low conductivity. A “perfect insulator” has \(k=0\), but a practical insulator like foam has \(k\approx 0.03\)~W/m\(\cdot\)K. Air is a good insulator (indeed, foam is mostly air) with \(k=0.026\)~W/m\(\cdot\)K, but one must be careful to not induce natural convection (which would increase heat transfer).

parameters

Parameters are things that can adopt different values, but that tend to be kept constant within a problem. Examples are: material properties like density \(\rho\) and viscosity \(\mu\), sizes like length \(L\) and diameter \(D\), and usually surface \(T_\text{surf}\) or ambient \(T_\infty\) temperatures. Technically all of these are described by symbols, but the values tend to stay constant within a problem. We know we are using water, we know the incoming air is at a controlled temperature, etc.

variables

Variables can adopt different values within an analysis, e.g.: how much more heat transfer \(\dot{Q}\) will we get if we double the incoming flow speed \(U\)? Both \(\dot{Q}\) and \(U\) are variables in this problem. I might use a notation like \(\dot{Q}(U;\rho,\mu)\), with parameters after the ;, to make clear that the value of \(\dot{Q}\) technically depends on \(\rho\) and \(\mu\) but the primary variation we are examining is with respect to \(U\).

thermal conductivity

A material property that emerges from the empirical Fourier’s Law. SI units: (Watts)/(meter Kelvin). Note that the Kelvin here is related to a difference in temperature, and \(\Delta T = 1~\text{K} = 1^\circ\text{C}\). That is, no unit transformation is necessary to go between W/m\(\cdot\)K and W/m\(\cdot^\circ\) C.

thermal diffusivity

A material property .

thermal inertia

title: Intro
text:
Notational conventions
I encourage you to adopt these conventions and be consistent with them.
When writing by hand, I underline my vectors; when typed, vectors are bold and not italicized.
If a function is written as $

title: 1  Conduction Eqn
text:
1.1 Think First
This week we are going to focus on conductivity, the dominant mode of heat transfer within solids. The basic picture in your head can be a rod where heat is being applied to one end and removed from the other. That is, heat is “flowing” through the solid. Conduction occurs in liquids and gases as well, but convection is more significant. And radiation occurs at the surfaces of all of them.
Starting with Fourier’s Law, we will derive the conductivity equation, a PDE (partial differential equation… but don’t worry). This equation will allow us to solve for temperature distributions as a function of space and time, e.g.: \(T(x,y,z,t)\). We use that to derive an important approach: the “thermal resistance” model for planar, cylindrical, and spherical geometries. This is a productive way of thinking about problems and will be something that we keep coming back to.
For organization reasons, I put some introductory content in an intro page. You should watch/read through that section, then come back here.
Recall
Look Ahead

title: 1  Conduction Eqn
text:
How many boundary conditions are needed to solve a second-order differential equation?
What does the Second Law of Thermodynamics say about the direction of heat flow?



How can we mathematically ingrain the truth that heat flows from hot to cold?
Can we consider time variation of temperature in our material? What if there is a nuclear reaction generating some heat? What if the thermal conductivity varies with temperature or position?
Are we going to need to solve a bunch of differential equations to get the heat transfer through a composite wall (one with multiple materials)?

title: 1  Conduction Eqn
text:
1.2 Fourier’s Law






TipVideo



Start with this video, which covers Fourier’s Law and the Conduction Equation. Then read the associated sections.


We have an empirical law that describes heat conduction known as Fourier’s Law. Stated mathematically:


“Empirical laws” are learned/guessed from experimental data… we can’t derive them. In the case of Fourier’s Law, we at least have a bit of the Second Law of Thermodynamics in there: heat flows from hot to cold. But otherwise, we guessed that the heat flux would be proportional to the temperature gradient, and invented a material property called “the thermal conductivity” that is basically the constant of proportionality.
\[
\begin{align}
q''&=-k \frac{dT}{dx}\\
\mathbf{q}'' &= -k \boldsymbol{\nabla} T.
\end{align}
\]
where the first equation is when there is one dimension to the heat flow, and the second is the more general three-dimensional version.



Symbol
Meaning
Units




\(T\)
always temperature in this class
K or °C


\(q''\)
heat flux
W/m²


\(k\)
thermal conductivity, a material property
W/m·K


\(dT/dx\) or \(\boldsymbol{\nabla}\)
temperature gradient
K/m



The general three-dimensional version of Fourier’s Law involves the gradient \(\boldsymbol{\nabla}\), and so it is a vector equation: the heat flux \(\mathbf{q}''\) is a vector. The thermal conductivity is a material property… for foam, a really good “insulator”, its value is about $k=$0.03 W/m·K. For copper, a really good “heat conductor”, its value is about $k=$400 W/m·K.






Important



The gradient is essentially a temperature difference as we change position, and a temperature difference of 5 K is the same as a temperature difference of 5°C. The thermal conductivity is written in as “per change in temperature”, and so it does not matter if you are dealing with Kelvin or degrees Celsius: \(k=\) 15 W/m·K = 15 W/m·°C. (Per-Kelvin is more standard.)



1.2.1 The significance of the gradient and minus sign
The “heat flux” \(\mathbf{q}''\) is basically the local heat transfer rate per area \(\dot{Q}/A\) (in the continuum limit). Note that it is proportional to the gradient in temperature \(\boldsymbol{\nabla}T\). Recall that the gradient of a scalar field (like temperature) tells us the direction of greatest increase. The negative sign in Fourier’s Law tells us that heat flows in the direction of greatest decrease in temperature. That is: heat flows from hot to cold.


1.2.2 The thermal conductivity
The thermal conductivity is a material property. Its value changes even with subtle changes in the material: 6061 aluminum has \(k=150\) W/m·K, while 3003 aluminum has \(k=180\) W/m·K. Moreover, it is a property that is highly dependent on temperature. In this class, we will usually ignore the temperature dependence and will call \(k\) a constant that we evaluate at an average temperature.
There are interesting design opportunities involving materials where \(k\) has high variation (e.g., a conductivity that dramatically increases with temperature), or where \(k\) is direction dependent (e.g. conductivity occurs more readily in the vertical direction owing to the printing of special, orientable molecules), or where composite materials are used (such that different sections of the device have different values of \(k\)).

title: 1  Conduction Eqn
text:
1.3 The Conduction Equation
Think back to the First Law of Thermodynamics: the Conservation of Energy. We’ll apply it to a section of a solid rod (the dotted rectangle below) and assume that the energy of this section can change due to: heat conduction in, heat conduction out, and heat generation within (e.g. due to chemical or nuclear reaction).


We will be dealing with the Conservation of Energy, written in a few different ways, in almost every aspect of this course.

  Conduction control volume
  A solid bar shown as two horizontal lines, with a dashed-border control volume in the center. An arrow labeled Q-dot-conduction(x) enters the left face and an arrow labeled Q-dot-conduction(x+Delta x) exits the right face. Q-dot-generation is labeled inside the box, and Delta x labels the width below.
  
    
      
    
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
    \(\dot{Q}_\text{conduction}(x)\)
  
  
    \(\dot{Q}_\text{conduction}(x+\Delta x)\)
  
  
    \(\dot{Q}_\text{generation}\)
  
  
    \(\Delta x\)
  

\[
\frac{dE}{dt} = \dot{Q}_\text{conduction,in} - \dot{Q}_\text{conduction,out} + \dot{Q}_\text{generation}.
\]
I’ll include the detailed derivation in the collapsed text below. The summary is:

We call on Fourier’s Law for the conduction terms at \(x\) and \(x+\Delta x\).
We rewrite the heat generation term as a generic \(\dot{Q}_\text{generated} = V \dot{q}\). That is, \(\dot{q}\) is the heat generated per volume at a certain point.
We rewrite the energy change term via the thermodynamic model that a change in energy is proportional to the associated change in temperature via the heat capacity \(mc\), where \(m\) is the mass and \(c\) is the specific heat (at constant volume). That is: \(dE = mc dT\).



The quantity \(mc\) is sometimes called the “thermal inertia”.






NoteClick here to see the details of the derivation.





For the conduction terms, we bring in the 1D version of Fourier’s Law, making sure to include the cross-sectional area \(A_\text{c}\) (remember, the heat flux \(q''\) is “heat transfer per area”). For the \(\dot{Q}_\text{generation}=V\dot{q}\) term, we’ll also write the volume as \((\Delta x) A_c\) and the mass as \(m=\rho V=\rho (\Delta x)A_c\) (where \(\rho\) is the density). Then we’ve got \[
\rho (\Delta x)A_c c\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \left(-kA_c \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg\rvert_x \right) - \left(-kA_c \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\bigg\rvert_{(x+\Delta x)}\right)+(\Delta x)(A_c) \dot{q}(x).
\]
Almost there! Divide through by \((\Delta x)A_c\) and swap the order of the conduction terms to leave
\[
\rho (\Delta x)A_c c\frac{\partial T}{\partial t}=\frac{k\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}(x+\Delta x)-k\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}(x)}{\Delta x} + \dot{q}
\]
And then recognize that, if we take the limit as \(\Delta x\rightarrow 0\), the fraction in that equation is the definition of derivative



We land here, with the one-dimensional conduction equation \[
\boxed{\rho c \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) + \dot{q}(x,t)}
\]
and, thinking back to calculus 3, it is intuitive that the full three-dimensional conduction equation will be
\[
\boxed{\rho c \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \boldsymbol{\nabla} \cdot \left(k \boldsymbol{\nabla}T\right) + \dot{q}}.
\]
Note that the heat generation term can be a function of location and/or time. In general, the conduction equation is a PDE for \(T(x,y,z,t)\) (if the problem is best phrased in Cartesian coordinates) or \(T(r,\theta,z,t)\) (if cylindrical). Our goal is to determine the spatial and temporal distribution of temperature.

1.3.1 A couple quick examples
… We’ll do more in the next section

Example 1.1 (Steady flow, constant conductivity, no heat generation, 1D Cartesian.) The geometry is an infinite slab where \(T(x=0)=T_0\) is known and \(T(x=L)=T_L\) is known. (These are the boundary conditions.) Then the Conduction Equation simplifies to
\[
\frac{d^2T}{dx^2} = 0
\]
Integrating twice will leave us with \(T(x) = c_1 x + c_2\). Applying the boundary conditions:
\[
T(x) = -\left(\frac{T_0 - T_L}{L}\right)x + T_0.
\]






Tip



You can work out the math in detail on your own if you want to check. You can also note that this equation is of the form \(T(x) = c_1 x + c_2\) (i.e., it solves the differential equation) and it satisfies the two boundary conditions.




Example 1.2 (Unsteady, with constant heat generation of \(\dot{q}_0\), and we’ll assume the object is small such that the spatial variation is negligible.) We’ll assume the object starts at a known temperature \(T(t=0)=T_0\). Then the Conduction Equation simplifies to
\[
\rho c \frac{dT}{dt} = \dot{q}_0
\]
Integrating once will leave us with \(T(t) = \frac{\dot{q}_0}{\rho c}t + d_1\), and the unknown constant \(d_1\) comes from the initial condition:
\[
T(t) = \frac{\dot{q}_0}{\rho c}t + T_0
\]






Tip



Check your thermodynamics intuition: if a constant heat generation is happening, will the object heat up quicker if it is a big specific heat, or a small specific heat?




Example 1.3 (Composite planar wall) This problem will motivate the thermal resistance section. Suppose we have this situation: a “composite wall” made of materials with different thermal conductivities, \(k_1\) and \(k_2\). The left and right boundaries have known temperatures \(T_\text{left}\) and \(T_\text{right}\). It is steady, no heat generation. How should we approach?

  Composite wall diagram
  Two rectangular layers representing a two-material composite wall. The left layer has width L1 and the right layer has width L2, both the same height. T-left labels the left face temperature and T-right labels the right face temperature.
  
  
  
    Tleft
  
  
    Tright
  
  
    \(k_1\)
  
  
    \(k_2\)
  
  
    L1
  
  
    L2
  







Important



The Conduction Equation applies to a single material: there is no way to handle the abrupt change in \(k\) that occurs at \(x=L_1\) using a single equation.


So we will need to solve the Conduction Equation twice. Actually, it is the same solution twice (see Example 1.1 above): \(T_1(x) = c_1 x + d_1\) and \(T_2(x) = c_2 x + d_2\). But what are the boundary conditions? It seems we need four…
\(T_1(0)=T_\text{left}\) is obvious, as is \(T_2(L_1+L_2)=T_\text{right}\). We need two more.
Physical observation #1: If the two rectangles are in perfect contact at \(x=L_1\) (more on this later), they must have the same temperature. We don’t know what that temperature is, but we can say \(T_1(L_1)=T_2(L_1)\).
Physical observation #2: In order for the system to be at steady state, the amount of heat entering material 1 must be the same as the amount of heat leaving material 1 (else the rectangle 1 would be increasing in temperature). And the amount of heat entering material 2 must be the same as the amount of heat leaving material 2 (same reason). Assuming \(T_\text{left}>T_\text{right}\), the heat leaving rectangle 1 must be entering rectangle 2. Framing this in terms of Fourier’s Law:
\[
-k_1 \frac{dT_1}{dx}\bigg\rvert_{L_1} = -k_2 \frac{dT_2}{dx}\bigg\rvert_{L_1}.
\]
A little bit messy, but that is our fourth boundary condition!
The algebra is tedious… you can go through it if you like, but I’m going to skip it for now.






TipThink



We just described two linear profiles, \(T_1(x)\) and \(T_2(x)\). Combined, they are continuous but are not smooth at \(x=L_1\). Intuitively (i.e., don’t just look at the equations): if \(k_1 > k_2\), is the slope of the temperature profile larger in material 1 or 2? What does that tell you about the temperature drop through insulators vs. through conductors? Does that make sense?

title: 1  Conduction Eqn
text:
1.4 Thermal Resistance






TipVideo



Start with this video, which covers the thermal resistance model.



1.4.1 The Ohm’s Law Metaphor
As engineers, we probably don’t care about the temperature at every location within an object. So solving coupled differential equations to get detailed temperature distributions within composite systems, as we did in Example 1.3, is practically not that useful. Instead, we are going to develop a metaphor with Ohm’s Law of electrical resistance, and use that to connect big design variables that engineers do care about: the total temperature difference and the total heat flow.
Recalling your electrical circuits class, Ohm’s Law is
\[
(\text{voltage}) = (\text{current})(\text{resistance}) \implies V=IR.
\]
We will show in this section that we can create a mathematical metaphor with heat transfer: current is our heat transfer rate \(\dot{Q}\), voltage is a temperature difference \(\Delta T\), and the “thermal resistance” is a function of things like thermal conductivity \(k\), cross-section area \(A_\text{c}\), and material thickness \(L\). That is:
\[
V=IR \implies \Delta T = \dot{Q} R_\text{thermal}
\]
or, rearranging and dropping the “thermal” subscript
\[
\dot{Q} = \frac{\Delta T}{R}.
\]
All the same Ohm’s Law rules apply: resistors in series sum together: \(R_\text{tot} = R_1 + R_2\). Resistors in parallel have the rule
\[
\frac{1}{R_\text{tot}}=\frac{1}{R_1}+\frac{1}{R_2}.
\]


1.4.2 Assumptions
For this section we’ll use:

Steady-state: \(\frac{\partial}{\partial t} = 0\)
Constant conductivity: \(\nabla \cdot (k\nabla T) = k\nabla^2 T\)
One-dimensional: \(\nabla^2 T = \frac{d^2T}{dx^2}\) (Cartesian) or \(=\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dT}{dr}\right)\) (Cylindrical) or \(=\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2 \frac{dT}{dr}\right)\) (Spherical)
No heat generation: \(\dot{q} = 0\)



1.4.3 Planar Wall (Infinite)
Recall the solution from Example 1.1:
\[
T(x) = -\left(\frac{T_0 - T_L}{L}\right)x + T_0.
\]
Using Fourier’s Law to compute the heat flux:
\[
q'' = -k\frac{dT}{dx} = k\left(\frac{T_0 - T_L}{L}\right).
\]






Note



If \(T_0>T_L\) we have that \(q''>0\). This is meaningful: heat will flow from left to right, in the positive \(x-\)direction. If \(T_0<T_L\), \(q''<0\), which implies heat flow in the negative \(x-\)direction.


To create the Ohm’s Law metaphor, we multiply both sides by cross-section area \(A_\text{c}\)
\[
q''A_\text{c} = \dot{Q} = kA_\text{c}\left(\frac{T_0 - T_L}{L}\right) = \frac{\Delta T}{L/(kA_\text{c})}.
\]
That is:
\[
\dot{Q} = \frac{\Delta T}{L/(kA_\text{c})} = \frac{\Delta T}{R_\text{planar}}
\]
and our “thermal resistance” for the planar wall geometry is
\[
R_\text{planar} \equiv L/(kA_\text{c}).
\]


1.4.4 “Composite” Wall
This is where the resistor model becomes really valuable: thinking back to Example 1.3, we know the total temperature drop across the system (\(\Delta T = T_\text{left}-T_\text{right}\)). We can write a thermal circuit with two resistors in series:



series circuit


and we can compute the total heat transfer through this wall easily
\[
\dot{Q} = \frac{\Delta T}{R_\text{total}} = \frac{T_\text{left}-T_\text{right}}{L_1/(k_1 A_1)+L_2/(k_2 A_2)}.
\]


1.4.5 Expanding the Metaphor
Two concepts that expand the utility of this metaphor:
Convection: we saw in our introduction to the three modes of heat transfer that
\[
\dot{Q}_\text{convection} = h A_\text{surface} (T-T_\infty) \implies \dot{Q}_\text{convection} = \frac{\Delta T}{1/(hA_\text{surface})}.
\]
That is,
\[
R_\text{convection} = 1/(h A_\text{surface}).
\]
Contact Resistance
If two rigid materials are joined together, it is unlikely that they achieve perfect contact. Rather, there may be small air gaps owing to machining imperfections, surface roughness, etc. Air is a good insulator (low \(k\)), and so this “contact resistance” will inhibit heat transfer.
We can continue the Ohm’s Law metaphor \[
\dot{Q}=\frac{\Delta T}{R_\text{contact}}
\]
but the values of contact resistance are given on a “per area” basis for the material pairings:
\[
R_\text{contact}=R''_\text{contact}/A_c.
\]
(The units of \(R''\) are m²⋅K/W, so we divide by the area to get the thermal resistance in K/W.) An example: for a ceramic-ceramic interface, we might have \(R''_\text{contact}=0.001\) m²⋅K/W. Contact resistance also is a function of pressure – pushing surfaces together will squish any imperfections and roughness, decreasing the amount of contact resistance. So: make sure all your bolts are tight!
Adding contact resistance to the composite wall simply involves adding a third resistor in series.






Note



If we were to sketch the temperature profiles \(T(x)\), the contact resistance would give a discontinuous jump in the temperature.


It is sometimes worthwhile to add thermal paste to the contact locations. This fills the gap with a high thermal conductivity material instead of air, improving good heat transfer.

title: 1  Conduction Eqn
text:
1.5 Interactive Example
I created an interactive example for a composite wall, find it at this link.

title: 1  Conduction Eqn
text:
1.6 Radial Systems
Let’s start with some intuition. Suppose we have the following cylindrical-shell geometry



concentric cylinders with heat flux


and we will assume angular and \(z-\)symmetry, and steady state. If it is indeed steady state, the amount of heat entering the shell must match the amount of heat leaving the shell: \(\dot{Q}_\text{in} = \dot{Q}_\text{out}\). But what can we say about the heat fluxes? Is it that \(q''_\text{in} = q''_\text{out}\), or \(q''_\text{in} > q''_\text{out}\), or \(q''_\text{in} < q''_\text{out}\).






NoteClick here to see the answer





\[
\left|q''(r_\text{in}) \cdot A_\text{in}\right| = \left|q''(r_\text{out}) \cdot A_\text{out}\right|
\]
and so \(q''_\text{in} > q''_\text{out}\).




1.6.1 Cylindrical
For both cylindrical and spherical coordinates, Fourier’s Law for radial heat flow is written as
\[
q''=-k \frac{dT}{dr}.
\]
But, unlike in the planar case, \(q''\) changes as we proceed through the material. We can still use an Ohm’s Law metaphor though!
We will solve the same problem as in the planar case: steady heat conduction with known temperature at the boundaries: \(T(r_\text{in})=T_\text{in}\) and \(T(r_\text{out})=T_\text{out}\). I’ll add the derivation inside here, and it may be useful to to look through it as this is an example of solving a differential equation in cylindrical coordinates.






NoteClick here to see the derivation





Start from the conduction equation with \(k = \text{constant}\), and we will assume steady heat transfer and no heat generation. Then we have, in cylindrical coordinates
\[
0=\nabla^2 T = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{\partial T}{\partial r}\right) + \underbrace{\frac{1}{r^2}\frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial \theta^2}}_{\to 0, symmetry} + \underbrace{\frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial z^2}}_{\to 0, symmetry}
\]
that is
\[
0=\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left(r\frac{d T}{d r}\right).
\]
Solving this by integration, we find
\[
T(r)=c_1 \ln r + c_2
\]
and applying the boundary conditions:
\[
T_\text{in} = c_1 \ln r_\text{in} + c_2
\]
\[
T_\text{out} = c_1 \ln r_\text{out} + c_2.
\]
Algebra: \[
T_\text{out} - T_\text{in} = c_1 \ln\left(\frac{r_\text{out}}{r_\text{in}}\right) \rightarrow  c_1 = \frac{T_\text{out} - T_\text{in}}{\ln(r_\text{out}/r_\text{in})}
\]
and
\[
c_2 = T_\text{in} - \frac{T_\text{out} - T_\text{in}}{\ln(r_\text{out}/r_\text{in})} \ln r_\text{in}
\]
gets us this:
\[
T(r) = T_\text{in} + \frac{T_\text{out} - T_\text{in}}{\ln(r_\text{out}/r_\text{in})} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{r}{r_\text{in}}\right).
\]
Then the heat flux is
\[
q''(r) = -k\frac{dT}{dr} = -k \frac{T_\text{out} - T_\text{in}}{\ln(r_\text{out}/r_\text{in})} \cdot \frac{1}{r}
\]
and the total heat transfer rate is
\[
|\dot{Q}| = |q''(r)| \cdot A(r) = k\frac{T_\text{out} - T_\text{in}}{\ln(r_\text{out}/r_\text{in})} \cdot \frac{1}{r} \cdot 2\pi r \cdot w
\]
where \(w\) is the length in the \(z-\)direction.



We land here:
\[
\dot{Q} = k \frac{\Delta T}{\ln(r_\text{out}/r_\text{in})} \cdot 2\pi w
\]
and bring back the Ohm’s Law metaphor:
\[
\dot{Q}= \frac{\Delta T}{R_\text{cylindrical}}
\]
and so we see
\[
R_\text{cylindrical}\equiv \frac{\ln(r_\text{out}/r_\text{in})}{2\pi k w}
\]


1.6.2 Spherical Case
Same process for spherical – steady, angular symmetry, known inner and outer temperatures. The Laplacian operator in spherical (with radial symmetry) is
\[
0 = \frac{1}{r^2}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r^2 \frac{dT}{dr}\right)
\]
Solution process is the same, Fourier’s Law is \(q''=-k dT/dr\), so I’ll just skip to…
\[
\dot{Q} = |q''(r)| \cdot A(r) = \left(\frac{\Delta T}{\frac{1}{r_\text{in}} - \frac{1}{r_\text{out}}}\right) \cdot \frac{k}{r^2} \cdot 4\pi r^2 = \left(\frac{\Delta T}{\frac{1}{r_\text{in}} - \frac{1}{r_\text{out}}}\right) 4\pi k
\]
From which we see
\[
R_\text{sphere} = \frac{\frac{1}{r_\text{in}} - \frac{1}{r_\text{out}}}{4\pi k}.
\]


1.6.3 Applying these equations
We can have composite cylinders/spheres: concentric rings of different materials and different thicknesses. We can add convection on the interior and exterior (remembering that the surface area of a cylinder is \(A_\text{surf}=2\pi R L\) and of a sphere is \(A_\text{surf}=4\pi R^2\)). We can write resistors in series, and if we know the interior and exterior temperatures, we can determine the total amount of heat transfer out of these configurations. Nice.
